food_subgroup: Grilled whole fish / grilled sea bass

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general sea bass food product class; no specific Turkish-style grilled sea bass entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers sea bass as a species; grilled preparation modifier applied as closest defensible match. No exact Turkish-style grilled sea bass code exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for grilled sea bass as a food product in the clinical terminology.

preparation_method: seasoned with herbs and olive oil

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Dragon Fish at Sultan Palace is approximately 350 g, representing one whole grilled sea bass plated with lemon wedges and herbs.

nutrient_source.title: Fish, sea bass, mixed species, cooked, dry heat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15060)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for sea bass cooked by dry heat used as the primary nutrient basis. Values represent plain grilled sea bass per 100 g edible portion. Minor upward adjustment to sodium (+15 mg) applied to account for typical restaurant seasoning with salt and herbs; all other macros retained from USDA source. Olive oil contribution to fat is consistent with USDA dry-heat values. This is the closest approved-source match for Turkish-style grilled sea bass.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of protein-dominant fish and seafood foods — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database fish category entries
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for grilled sea bass in the University of Sydney GI Database. Grilled fish is a high-protein, negligible-carbohydrate food. Carbohydrate_g per 100 g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA dry-heat sea bass). GI is estimated at 5 (effectively negligible) consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database convention for protein-dominant, near-zero-carbohydrate fish foods. Glycemic load per 350 g serving = (5 × 0.0 × 3.5) / 100 = 0.0. gi_category set to low.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium from seasoning; high potassium content
hypertension.guidance: Grilled sea bass contains approximately 102 mg sodium per 100 g and 328 mg potassium per 100 g; the favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio makes it a suitable protein choice for hypertensive individuals when consumed without added salt sauces.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content; high-quality lean protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: With 0.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g and a glycemic load of 0.0 per serving, grilled sea bass has no meaningful glycemic impact and is an excellent lean protein option for blood glucose management in individuals with Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean fish with omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids; low saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Grilled sea bass provides 0.9 g polyunsaturated fat (including omega-3 fatty acids) and only 0.6 g saturated fat per 100 g, supporting cardiovascular health; regular consumption of lean grilled fish is consistent with heart-healthy dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dragon Fish is the menu name used by Sultan Palace Turkish and Lebanese Cuisine in Singapore for a whole grilled sea bass (Dicentrarchus labrax), a dish rooted in Turkish coastal culinary tradition. The fish is typically seasoned with olive oil, lemon juice, garlic, and Mediterranean herbs such as parsley, thyme, and oregano before being grilled whole over high heat, producing a lightly charred exterior with moist, flaky white flesh inside. The flavour profile is clean, mildly saline, and herbaceous, with a subtle smokiness from the grill. In Turkish cuisine, grilled sea bass (levrek ızgara) is a classic restaurant preparation served with lemon wedges, fresh salad, and sometimes rice or bread. Nutritionally, grilled sea bass is a high-protein (approximately 23.6 g per 100 g), low-fat (2.6 g per 100 g), and negligible-carbohydrate food, making it one of the most metabolically favourable protein sources available. It is rich in vitamin B12, selenium, phosphorus, and vitamin D. The glycemic load per serving is effectively zero, making it ideal for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or following low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. The low saturated fat content and presence of omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids support cardiovascular health. At SGD 27 per serving at Sultan Palace Boat Quay, it represents a premium restaurant fish dish. The dish is halal and gluten-free.
common_user_queries: What is Dragon Fish at Sultan Palace?
Is Dragon Fish good for diabetics?
Is Sultan Palace Dragon Fish halal?
Dragon Fish field survey Sultan Palace

confidence_reason: The food name 'Dragon Fish' is a restaurant menu name for grilled sea bass at Sultan Palace Singapore. No direct nutritional entry for this specific dish exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for sea bass cooked by dry heat (NDB 15060), which is the closest approved-source match. Confidence is medium because the USDA source is a strong approved secondary source for this fish species and preparation method, but minor restaurant-specific seasoning variations cannot be fully controlled.
notes: 1. carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for sea bass cooked by dry heat; this is a protein-dominant fish with no glycemic carbohydrates. sugar_g is confirmed zero for the same reason. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero as fish contains no dietary fibre. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — naturally absent in grilled fish at trace-or-below levels per USDA source). vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA source for cooked sea bass). 2. GI is set to 5 (effectively negligible) as an analogous estimate consistent with University of Sydney GI Database conventions for near-zero-carbohydrate fish foods; glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate_g is 0.0. 3. Sodium value of 102 mg per 100 g includes a +15 mg adjustment above the USDA baseline (87 mg) to account for typical restaurant salt seasoning. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.7), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.9), potassium_mg (328), calcium_mg (14), iron_mg (0.4), cholesterol_mg (53), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (15), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.12), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.08), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (2.5), vitamin_b6_mg (0.4), vitamin_b12_mcg (0.9), folate_mcg_dfe (5.0), vitamin_d_mcg (5.6), vitamin_e_mg (0.6), phosphorus_mg (211), magnesium_mg (34), zinc_mg (0.5), selenium_mcg (36.5), water_g (72.1) all sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 15060. 5. This record covers the Turkish cuisine category; multilingual names are provided for English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per Singaporean dish multilingual rules, as this dish is served in Singapore. Turkish romanized alias (Ejder Balığı, Levrek Izgara) also provided. 6. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03301116 is the sea bass food product class; match_type is closest_parent as no grilled-preparation-specific class exists. FoodEx2 A00LC is the sea bass species code; match_type is closest_parent.
